agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with clear intent (demand-based pricing, automation, guardrails) but leaves most specifics undefined. This is a pre-spec, vision-stage description.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product/system that performs real-time dynamic price adjustments for deliveries based on demand signals"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity — undefined whether this is per-zone, per-time-window, or global"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or unreasonable pricing outcomes"},{"term":"Delivery Price","definition":"The price charged to a customer for a delivery, which DynaPricing will adjust dynamically"},{"term":"Real-time adjustment","definition":"Price changes that occur continuously or near-continuously in response to demand signals, not batch/scheduled"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The direction of adjustment is a business policy. A VP could decide to absorb high-demand costs as a competitive strategy. The correlation between high demand and higher price is a policy choice, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand exceeds the high-demand threshold, the computed price must be greater than the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to baseline","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Lowering prices during low demand is an economic incentive policy. Management could choose to keep prices flat during low demand instead.","testable_assertion":"Given demand falls below the low-demand threshold, the computed price must be less than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time — not via batch, manual intervention, or scheduled updates","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is a product requirement and operational policy, not a data-integrity constraint. The business could fall back to manual pricing without corrupting the system.","testable_assertion":"Given a change in demand signal, the system must recompute and apply a new price within a defined latency SLA without human intervention"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The system must be fully automated — no human approval required for individual price changes","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation level is an operational policy. A human-in-the-loop override could be introduced without violating any physical or data constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand-triggered price change, the system must execute the adjustment end-to-end without requiring human approval"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from exceeding acceptable bounds (floor and/or ceiling)","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Guardrail thresholds are configurable business policies. The specific values are clearly meant to be tunable. However, the EXISTENCE of guardrails is strongly implied as a business requirement.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand level (including extreme spikes or zero demand), the computed price must remain within the configured minimum and maximum bounds"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The computed delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A delivery price of zero or negative is economically incoherent — the business cannot pay customers to take deliveries. This is a fundamental domain constraint: a price must represent a charge for a service rendered.","testable_assertion":"For any combination of demand inputs and pricing parameters, the final delivery price must be strictly greater than zero"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The pricing algorithm must consider both competitiveness and profitability — neither can be entirely sacrificed for the other","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a strategic policy that leadership adjusts. It is a business objective, not a system invariant.","testable_assertion":"Given the pricing configuration, there must exist both a competitive benchmark input and a profitability margin input, and the algorithm must reference both when computing a price"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., 100x normal) — what is the price ceiling?","expected_behavior":"Price must be capped at the configured guardrail maximum. Specific value is UNDEFINED in the spec."},{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero — what is the price floor?","expected_behavior":"Price must not drop below the configured guardrail minimum. Must remain positive. Specific value is UNDEFINED."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly (e.g., second-by-second fluctuation) — does the system chase every signal?","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED. The spec says 'real time' but does not define smoothing, dampening, or minimum adjustment intervals. Price instability is a real risk."},{"scenario":"Multiple geographic zones have vastly different demand simultaneously","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED. The spec does not clarify whether demand is measured globally, per-zone, per-route, or per-service-tier."},{"scenario":"The pricing system goes down or becomes unresponsive","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED. No fallback pricing strategy is specified. Should the system use the last known price? A default base price? Block orders?"},{"scenario":"A price guardrail ceiling is set below the profitability threshold","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED. The spec says both guardrails and profitability matter, but does not define which wins in a conflict."},{"scenario":"Regulatory price-gouging laws are triggered during a natural disaster or emergency","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED. Many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging statutes. The spec makes no mention of legal/regulatory compliance constraints on dynamic pricing."},{"scenario":"Customer sees one price, confirms order, but demand changed during checkout","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED. No mention of price-lock windows, quote validity periods, or honoring displayed prices."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue and margin. Upside: capture surplus during high demand, stimulate volume during low demand. Downside: miscalibrated guardrails or algorithms could erode margins or drive away price-sensitive customers.","operational":"Fully automated system requires robust monitoring, alerting, and fallback mechanisms. Pricing errors propagate instantly at scale with no human buffer. Operational burden shifts from manual pricing to algorithm governance and guardrail tuning.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Surge-pricing backlash (as seen in ride-sharing) is a known risk. Price consistency expectations in logistics differ from ride-hailing.","risk":"HIGH. Regulatory risk from price-gouging laws is unaddressed. Reputational risk from perceived unfair pricing. Financial risk from algorithm errors at scale with no human approval gate. Competitive risk if guardrails are too conservative or too aggressive.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Will see variable delivery prices — may perceive unfairness during high-demand periods. Need transparency on why prices change."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Must understand that pricing is automated. May need visibility into current pricing state for customer support escalations."},{"group":"Finance","impact":"Revenue becomes less predictable. Need reporting on pricing distribution, margin impact, and guardrail hit rates."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive complaints about price variability. Need tooling to explain prices and potentially override or credit."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review pricing bounds for regulatory compliance, especially anti-price-gouging and consumer protection laws."},{"group":"Sales/Partnerships","impact":"Enterprise or contract customers may expect fixed pricing — DynaPricing scope for B2B vs B2C is undefined."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Must build a low-latency, high-reliability pricing engine with monitoring and fallback strategies."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"This is a CEO vision statement from an all-hands meeting, not a product specification. It contains exactly five sentences with no quantitative thresholds, no defined demand model, no guardrail values, no geographic scoping, no fallback strategy, no regulatory consideration, no price-lock semantics, and no stakeholder-specific carve-outs. Nearly every extracted rule required inference beyond what was explicitly stated. The only high-confidence extraction is directional: prices go up with demand, down without, and guardrails exist.","high_confidence_areas":["Demand-price positive correlation (BR-1, BR-2)","System must be automated (BR-3, BR-4)","Guardrails must exist in some form (BR-5)","Price must be positive (BR-6)"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific guardrail values (floor/ceiling) — completely undefined","Demand measurement granularity (global, zone, route, time-window)","Fallback behavior when pricing system is unavailable","Price-lock or quote validity during checkout","Regulatory and legal compliance constraints","Competitiveness vs profitability tradeoff resolution","Whether B2B/contract customers are in scope","Smoothing or dampening of rapid demand oscillation"],"assumptions":["Demand is a measurable, quantifiable signal available to the system","There exists a concept of a 'base price' against which adjustments are made","Guardrails imply at minimum a price ceiling; likely also a floor","The system applies to customer-facing delivery prices, not internal cost allocation","Real-time means sub-minute latency, not daily or hourly batch"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The spec does not define what 'demand' means quantitatively, how it is measured, or at what granularity (per-zone, per-route, per-time-window, globally). Without this, no pricing algorithm can be correctly specified.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Finance"],"options":["Define demand as order volume per zone per time window","Define demand as capacity utilization ratio","Define demand as a composite signal including forecasted and real-time inputs"],"recommendation":"Require Product to define a formal demand model with units, granularity, and data sources before any implementation begins."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Guardrail values (price floor, price ceiling, maximum percentage change) are not specified. The CEO says 'probably' and 'so prices don't go crazy' — this is not a spec.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Legal","Customers"],"options":["Define fixed absolute floor and ceiling","Define percentage-based bounds relative to base price","Define both absolute and percentage bounds with the tighter constraint winning"],"recommendation":"Require Finance and Legal to jointly define guardrail bounds before launch. This is non-negotiable for regulatory safety."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No fallback strategy is defined for when the pricing engine is unavailable. In a fully automated system, this is a critical gap.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["Use last known computed price","Fall back to a static base price","Block new orders until pricing is restored"],"recommendation":"Define a fallback pricing strategy as a Day 1 requirement. 'Use base price as fallback' is the safest default."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of regulatory compliance. Dynamic pricing in delivery/logistics may trigger anti-price-gouging laws in many jurisdictions, especially during emergencies or natural disasters.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal","Customers","Executive"],"options":["Add jurisdiction-aware pricing caps","Add emergency-mode override that disables surge pricing","Conduct legal review before launch"],"recommendation":"MANDATORY legal review before any launch. This is a liability risk that cannot be deferred."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No mention of price-lock or quote validity. If a customer sees a price and demand changes during checkout, it is undefined whether the displayed price is honored.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Engineering","Customer Support"],"options":["Lock price at display time for a configurable window (e.g., 5 minutes)","Always charge the price at order confirmation time","Lock price at cart-add, re-validate at confirmation"],"recommendation":"Lock price at display time for a reasonable window. Charging a different price than displayed destroys customer trust and may violate consumer protection laws."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"This description is insufficient to define acceptance criteria","business_rule_broken":"No specific business rule is broken because no specific rules have been formally defined. The CEO's vision statement lacks the precision needed to build a correct system. Acceptance criteria are intentionally left empty because defining them from this input would be fabrication, not extraction."}]}
